content:
Hi,
I currently try to register a new class, with this C macro: https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/include/linux/device/class.h#L260

I can't find an implementation of this in the rust-for-linux for, and no issue related to this.

So, right now I use some unsafe:

unsafe {bindings::__class_create(module.0, name.as_char_ptr(), KEY.get())}

But I have to made things public in ThisModule and LockClassKey to make this working.

Did I miss the implementation of class_create ? If no, is there a better way to get *mut module and *mut lock_class_key than making things public in the kernel crate ?

A tricky part for this is class_create macro allocates a static location for lock_class_key, so to behave as the same, you will need introduce a Rust macro, for example

macro_rules! class_create {
  (owner:$expr, name:$expr) => {{
    static key: LockClassKey = ...;
    // create local binding to avoid evaluate unknown expression in `unsafe`
    let owner = $owner;
    let name = $name;
    unsafe {bindings::__class_create(owner, name.as_char_ptr(), KEY.get())}
  }}
}

Yes, but I still need to put LockClassKey.get and ThisModule.0 public to use this macro
I think I need to create some "safe" wrapper on class to avoid this, but I really don't know where and how to begin :sweat_smile:

I think LockClassKey::get() is both public and safe, so maybe you can define a "constructor" for Class:

impl Class {
  pub fn new(name: &'static CStr, key: &'static LockClassKey) -> Self {
    let mut owner = Class { .. };

    unsafe {bindings::__class_create(owner.0, name.as_char_ptr(), key.get())}
    owner
  }
}

pub struct Class {
    pub(crate) ptr: *mut bindings::class,
}

impl Class {
    pub fn new( owner: &'static ThisModule, name: &'static CStr,key: &'static LockClassKey) -> Result<Self> {
        // SAFETY: all pointers are guaranteed valid by compiler
        let ptr = from_kernel_err_ptr(
            unsafe {bindings::__class_create(owner.0, name.as_char_ptr(), key.get())}
        )?;

        Ok(Self {ptr})
    }
}

impl Drop for Class {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // SAFETY: As the class can only be created with `new`, ptr is valid
        unsafe {bindings::class_destroy(self.ptr)}
    }
}

Just before your message I come with this :smile:
I added the destructor because I need to call it on module unloading
I'm not sure about the SAFETY justfication, but at least it seems to works
Looks good to me ;-)
See you at LKML I guess? ;-)

Welllll, maybe, but I really don't know how to submit it :sweat_smile:
Do you have a guide to do this properly ?

Hmm.. I missed that you wrap a pointer instead of a struct, please consider

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct Class(bindings::class);

pub struct ClassRef {
  ptr: NonNull<Class>
}
Thanks !

Louis Chauvet said:

Welllll, maybe, but I really don't know how to submit it :sweat_smile:
Do you have a guide to do this properly ?

There is https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html

For the NonNull creation, should I use:

  • new_unchecked (with unsafe), because I'm sure that the pointer is invalid after from_kernel_err_ptr
  • new with unwrap
  • new and check that the Option really contains something

Boqun Feng said:

Louis Chauvet said:

Welllll, maybe, but I really don't know how to submit it :sweat_smile:
Do you have a guide to do this properly ?

There is https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html

I'll have to check with my company's legal department, but I hope to have the right to submit it.
I think new_unchecked here is OK
Also please read https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing

Hello,
I have the authorization from my company to submit patches :partying_face:
On which branch should I base my work ? Latest mainstream or one of the rfl branch ? (which one in this case ?)
You can base your patches on the rust-next branch, or on Linus' Linux 6.4-rc1 commit.

Ho, I have a problem, I think my patch should go to this file: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/kernel/device.rs, but it's not yet upstream on linux 6.4

(It's a wrapper around https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/include/linux/device/class.h#L54)

Should I create this file with only my patch ? (It doesn't depend on struct Device, but as class is defined in the device folder in C, I think it's the "right" place. Or should I create device.rs to include device/class.rs ?)
It needs to be upstreamed first.

Louis Chauvet said:

Ho, I have a problem, I think my patch should go to this file: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/kernel/device.rs, but it's not yet upstream on linux 6.4

(It's a wrapper around https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/include/linux/device/class.h#L54)

Should I create this file with only my patch ? (It doesn't depend on struct Device, but as class is defined in the device folder in C, I think it's the "right" place. Or should I create device.rs to include device/class.rs ?)

Just submit your patch the way you feel is right and then we can talk about if it needs to be moved around when we do the review :) If you need to create a file or module, do that.

No, please wait until dependencies are upstreamed (or submitted to the list).

For device.rs, @Wedson Almeida Filho is working on submitting it as far as I understand, so as soon as he submits the patch(es), you can rebase on top of those and submit yours.

However, if you are looking for early feedback, then you may want to create a branch somewhere with your dependencies, and then submit the patch based on your branch, and mark it as an RFC series for instance.
Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules for some other tips on that.

Miguel Ojeda said:

No, please wait until dependencies are upstreamed (or submitted to the list).

For device.rs, Wedson Almeida Filho is working on submitting it as far as I understand, so as soon as he submits the patch(es), you can rebase on top of those and submit yours.

He says it does not depend on anything in device.rs but that file would be a good place for his stuff as well. It goes without saying "don't submit other peoples stuff".
I would encourage sending it to the list, slap an RFC badge on it and get some feedback sooner rather than later.

Ah, I read it as upstreaming parts of the existing file (even if not struct Device).

If it does not depend on anything that is not in rust-next and it is just a matter of placement, then Andreas is right -- definitely please go ahead!

Ok, I tried to go ahead, but I didn't expect from_kernel_err_ptr to not be upstreamed :sweat_smile:

  • If I look at rustforlinux/rust-next to create a class I need to use bindings::class_create (linux 6.4) (but I don't have from_kerne_err_ptr)
  • If I look at rustforlinux/rust, I need to use bindings::__class_create (linux 6.3) (but this must be updated when you bump the linux version)
    What should I do ? Wait the next version bump in rustforlinux/rust ?

Regarding the Linux version, you can ignore the rust branch -- patches go for mainline. Indeed, Greg changed it recently in several ways (removed the THIS_MODULE parameter need from the macro, then made it a function and removed the key), which makes it a bit easier too.

Regarding from_kernel_err_ptr: it got renamed to from_err_ptr when it got upstreamed recently, so you should have it.
There's an open patch for lock_class_key now so you should be able to base off that
